---
title: "Children's Expectations of Emotional Intimacy in Close Relationships"
author:
  - name: "[Author names removed for anonymous review]"
date: today
abstract: |
  Humans across cultures distinguish intimate, close, or family-like
  relationships from those that are merely affiliative. Recent work suggests that
  infants and children recognize one common cue of such relationships, physical
  intimacy. The current studies investigate how children, ages 6 to 9 years, and
  adults think about another hallmark of intimate relationships: emotional
  intimacy. In Study 1, children used the disclosure of sad emotions, but not disclosure
  facts or happy emotions, to infer who was 'best friends'. Adults used the disclosure of any emotion to infer who was 'best friends'. In Study 2, children and adults
  generally expected that people in close social relationships would disclose sad
  emotions over facts. In Study 3, neither children nor adults thought that
  disclosing sad emotions was a way to create social relationships or deepen
  them. In Study 4, children and adults expected a child to disclose sad emotions
  to their mother but not a friend's mother, and did not expect mothers to
  disclose sad emotions to children, revealing asymmetrical expectations about disclosure in
  caregiving relationships. Together, these results suggest that by the age of six
  years, children connect close social relationships with emotional intimacy, but
  they don't see disclosure as a way to create intimacy.
keywords: [social cognition, information sharing, emotional intimacy]
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
fig-align: center
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: false
  docx:
    output-file: OMIT_manuscript_rendered.docx
    reference-doc: apa-reference.docx
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
suppressMessages({ library(here); library(dplyr); library(readr) })

res     <- here::here("5. Analysis", "results")
fig_dir <- here::here("5. Analysis", "figures")

B <- read_csv(file.path(res, "binomial_BF.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(cell = coalesce(relationship, measure))
M <- read_csv(file.path(res, "model_estimates.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
WG <- read_csv(file.path(res, "whole_group_estimates.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

# % chose emotion for one cell
pct <- function(study, pop, cell, emo) {
  r <- dplyr::filter(B, study == !!study, population == pop, cell == !!cell, emotion == emo)
  paste0(round(r$pct_emo), "%")
}
# format a Bayes factor: whole number (comma-grouped) when large (>= 10),
# two decimals when small (< 10)
fmt_bf <- function(x) if (x >= 10) formatC(round(x), format = "d", big.mark = ",") else sprintf("%.2f", x)
# one-sided Bayes factor (BF10 if the hypothesised direction is supported, else BF01)
bf <- function(study, pop, cell, emo) {
  r <- dplyr::filter(B, study == !!study, population == pop, cell == !!cell, emotion == emo)
  if (r$BF10 >= 1) sprintf("$BF_{10}$ = %s", fmt_bf(r$BF10))
  else             sprintf("$BF_{01}$ = %s", fmt_bf(r$BF01))
}
# posterior median + 95% CI for a model term (model_estimates.csv: adults, age
# interactions, cell-means)
est <- function(model, term) {
  r <- dplyr::filter(M, model == !!model, term == !!term)
  sprintf("median = %.2f, 95%% CI [%.2f, %.2f]", r$median, r$CI_low, r$CI_high)
}
# whole-group (no-age) child condition effect (whole_group_estimates.csv)
wg <- function(study, term) {
  r <- dplyr::filter(WG, model == paste0(study, "_noage"), term == !!term)
  sprintf("median = %.2f, 95%% CI [%.2f, %.2f]", r$median, r$CI_low, r$CI_high)
}
```

**Word count:** approximately 8,100 (text and abstract; excludes title,
author note, references, tables, and figure captions).

# Public Significance Statement

These studies show that by elementary school, children already grasp
something adults take for granted---that confiding sadness, rather than
sharing neutral facts or good news, is a mark of a close relationship.
Across four studies, six- to nine-year-olds expected people to reveal
their sad feelings to best friends and to their own mothers, but not to
friends or a friend's parent, and they treated a child confiding in a
parent as different from a parent confiding in a child. Yet neither
children nor adults viewed opening up emotionally as a way to build a
new friendship, suggesting that people intuitively treat emotional
intimacy as belonging to relationships that are already close rather
than as a tool for creating closeness. These insights may help explain
why some children find it difficult to judge what to share, and with
whom.

# Introduction

Humans in every culture distinguish 'thick' relationships (strong,
intimate, close, or family-like relationships) from those that are
'thin' (weak, affiliative, prosocial) [@clark1979; @fiske1992]. This
distinction seems to be made as early as 8 months: infants expect that
those who are physically intimate will respond to one another's distress
[@thomasSaliva2022 ]. One hallmark of such relationships is emotional
intimacy, which includes disclosing personal information. Emotional
intimacy has been studied across several disciplines, such as
Anthropology, Relationship Science [@finkenauer2015know;
@willems2020role], and Sociology. Sociologists have long characterized
relationships by the kind of information disclosed within them. More
than a century ago, @simmel1906sociology argued that what and how much
people reveal to one another is calibrated to the closeness of the
relationship: acquaintances share outward, impersonal facts, whereas
intimate bonds rest on the disclosure of one's inner life, including
emotions. Contemporary network research operationalizes a version of
this idea through the kinds of ties people report---weak ties are those
to whom one turns for useful information, while thick ties are those to
whom one discloses sensitive or personal matters [refer to @small2017,
for a history].

Yet, there have been few empirical studies that test whether people
reason that close social relationships include emotional disclosure, nor
how early in development this intuition may arise. In the present
studies, we ask, do adults and U.S. American children, ages 6 to 9
years, reason that emotional intimacy is part of close social
relationships? We operationalize emotional intimacy as revealing sad
emotions, as compared to happy emotions and facts. We reasoned that
telling someone else about one's sadness may make one more vulnerable or
may imply an expectation of support [e.g., @simmel1906sociology], which
people may intuit is more diagnostic of close social relationships than
sharing happy emotions or facts, which may be seen as generally
prosocial and applicable in many positive social relationships.

## Children's Intuitive Theories of Social Relationships

A growing body of research suggests that children have rich intuitive
theories of social relationships [@shutts2021; @thomsen2020] and that
many of these intuitions may have their roots in infancy [@thomas2024].
Children have rich intuitions about friendship for example, they expect
friends to play together, to act prosocially toward one another, and to
be more similar as opposed to non-friends [refer to @afshordi2021 for
review]. However there has been less work investigating how children
think about the distinction between intimate and less intimate social
partners (e.g., best friends and friends; family versus friends).

One study that investigated such a distinction found that while children
expect that others will share more resources with friends compared to
strangers and family compared to strangers, they do not reliably expect
more resource sharing with family compared to friends
[@thomasSaliva2022; @spokes2016], suggesting that they do not connect
social closeness with increased prosociality. Children do, however,
distinguish between family and friends when predicting who will be
physically intimate---for example sharing a single ice cream cone or a
single spoon---items that require saliva sharing[@thomasSaliva2022].
Thus, children do not think that sharing resources is an indicator of
close relationships but do think that physical intimacy is one. How
might children think about emotional intimacy, and in particular
disclosure within relationships? Do children distinguish between
intimate and non-intimate disclosure in the same way that they
distinguish between intimate and non-intimate sharing?

## Intuitions about Relationships and Information Sharing

Children do have some early developing intuitions about what kind of
information is disclosed within relationships. For example, 6-year-old
children recognize that information that may make someone look bad
(e.g., that someone took something that wasn't theirs) is more likely to
be kept as a secret and think that secret sharing is more likely to
occur with friends than strangers [@anagnostaki2013]. Likewise, children
also expect that those who disclose secrets are more likely to be
friends than non-friends [@liberman2018].

More generally, children seem to believe that people in close
friendships will better know one another's minds: Children as young as 6
years expect that those who display physical intimacy (by touching and
dancing), compared to those who display affiliation (by dancing without
touching), will better know one another's mental states, such as
preferences, but not perceptually available attributes of the person.
They also expect that those who know each other's emotional states are
socially closer [@woo2024]. However this work does not tell us whether
children expect the disclosure of sensitive information, like emotional
states, is more likely to occur in close social relationships, or
whether they see it as diagnostic of close relationships.

Importantly, not all information disclosure should be equally indicative
of a close social relationship. Just as disclosing a secret with someone
may leave someone vulnerable, an individual's emotional state,
especially when negative, may carry its own risks. For example it could
lead to negative views of the discloser or imply that the listener is
expected to help. This vulnerability is plausibly why emotional
disclosure is reserved for close relationships where these risks are
mitigated. Prior work does suggest people perceive disclosure as
potentially costly. In one study, early adolescent girls, aged 10 to 13
years, forfeited money to disclose personal facts to close friends, but
gave up more money to disclose relatively superficial information (e.g.,
liking reality TV) than intimate information (e.g., feeling
self-conscious). They also shared intimate information less often
[@vijayakumar2020]. The authors interpret this reluctance to share
intimate information as adaptive: sharing highly personal information
can be inappropriate or disadvantageous in newer or less close
relationships even if it is central to very close ones. Consistent with
it being central in close social relationships, self-disclosure patterns
accounted for 72% of the variance in intimacy ratings among romantic
couples [@chelune1984], and college students said they disclose personal
information when they want to have close or trusting relationships or to
increase intimacy [@derlega2008]. While this mapping is not perfect--at
least in some contexts, people confide in 'thin' ties [@small2017]\--
emotional disclosure is frequently observed in and theorized to create
intimate relationships. In the current studies we focus on whether
people intuit that sharing negative states is especially indicative of
close social relationships, consistent with this literature, and whether
children may think about the relationship between social intimacy and
emotional disclosure.

On the one hand, children may not connect emotional disclosure with
intimacy. First, it is unclear how children think about the concept of
private emotions, let alone that disclosing these emotions might be part
of some but not all social relationships. While previous research
suggests children recognize secrets, in one study the secret was not
specified, and in another it involved something that a child could get
in trouble for. Disclosing emotions makes people vulnerable in a less
straightforward way, making it potentially less likely that children
would recognize it as a cue for close social relationships. Moreover, in
the current studies, we contrast disclosing emotions with disclosing
information, which could be seen as potentially prosocial because it
provides the other person with potentially useful information. According
to some theories of social relationships, relationship strength is based
on adopting another person's utility [@powell2022adopted], thus forgoing
the disclosure of interesting facts may be seen as antisocial and
indicative of *non close* relationships. We start with children aged 6
to 9 years because children at this age are adept at understanding that
others have thoughts, beliefs, and emotions that may differ from their
own [@wellman2015] and previous studies have found that this older age
group, but not younger children, believe that people in closer
relationships better know the content of one another's minds.

Importantly, this age range also reflects a transitional phase in
children's social lives. As they move through elementary school,
children broaden their social worlds, from primarily family-based
interactions to peer relationships formed in classrooms,
extracurriculars, and other group settings [@burke2022]. This expansion
provides opportunities for children to have a broader array of
relationships that vary in levels of intimacy and trust. Thus, children
in this age group may be especially attuned to the significance of
disclosing information.

In the current studies, our goal was to understand whether children
connect social closeness with disclosing information and whether their
intuitions were different from or the same as adults. We investigated
this kind of reasoning in four studies. In Study 1, we asked, do
children and adults infer social closeness based on the type of
information that someone discloses (emotions vs. facts and happy vs. sad
emotions)? In Study 2, we asked, do children expect people in a close
relationship (a best friend vs. a friend) to disclose sad emotions more
than facts? In Study 3, we asked, given the desire to create or deepen a
relationship, which type of information do children think will be
disclosed? In Study 4, we asked how children reason about emotional
disclosure in asymmetric (mother--child) relationships. @fig-overview
provides an overview of the design of all four studies.

```{r}
#| label: fig-overview
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Overview of the four studies. Each study probes the intuitive link between emotional disclosure (revealing a sad emotion rather than a neutral fact) and social closeness. Study 1 asks whether children and adults infer who is closer from what a character discloses to two others (and who the character would share a single ice cream cone or partitionable candy with). Study 2 asks, given a friend versus a best friend, who is expected to be told the emotion rather than the fact. Study 3 asks whether disclosing an emotion is seen as a way to create (3.A) or deepen (3.B) a friendship. Study 4 asks about an asymmetric caregiving relationship: whether a child is expected to disclose to their own mother versus a friend's mother (4.A), and whether a mother is expected to disclose to her child (4.B)."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "study_overview.png"))
```

# General Methods

## Participants

One group of participants participated in Studies 1, 4.A, and 3.A, which
occurred during the same session, and another group participated in
Studies 2, 3.B, and 4.B in the same session. Across all studies, we
counterbalanced the order of all relevant conditions.

For studies 1, 3.A, and 4.A, 57 U.S. American children aged 6 to 9 years
old (mean = 7.81 years, range = 6.04 to 9.88 years, S.D. = 1.05), were
tested via online video call. All participants participated in all three
studies during the same session. 36 parents reported their child was a
boy, and 21 parents reported their child was a girl. Participants were
recruited using a lab database, advertisements on social media, and
Children Helping Science (lookit.mit.edu; @scott2017lookit). 45% of
parents said their children were White or Caucasian, 26% reported Asian,
3% reported Black or African American, 17% reported multiracial, 1%
reported Middle Eastern, 1% reported South American, and 3% did not
report a race for their child. 28% of caregivers reported having a
Master's degree, 14% reported having a doctorate degree, 37% reported
having a Bachelor's degree, 5% reported having an Associate's degree, 1%
reported having completed elementary and high school, and 13% did not
report an education level. For comparison, we also surveyed 47 U.S.
American adult participants (mean age = 26.8 years old, 20 women, 27
men) through Prolific, an online platform. The adult participants saw
the same stories and questions as the children.

For studies 2, 3.B, and 4.B, we tested 105 children aged 6 to 9 years
old (mean = 7.81, range = 6.01 to 9.71 years, S.D. = 1.16); the first 75
were tested online over video call and the remainder were collected
asynchronously using the Children Helping Science website. All
participants took part in all three studies. 55 children were reported
to be girls, 49 were reported to be boys, and 1 non-binary. 50% of
parents said their children were White or Caucasian, 23% reported Asian,
19% reported multiracial, 4% reported Hispanic or Latine, 2% reported
Black or African American, 1% reported Middle Eastern, and 2% did not
report a race for their child. Across caregivers, the highest reported
education level was a Master's degree for 33%, a Bachelor's degree for
24%, a professional degree for 15%, a doctorate for 12%, an Associate's
degree for 5%, some college for 2%, high school for 1%, and elementary
for 1%; 7% did not report an education level. We also collected a sample
of 49 adults (mean age = 23.5 years old, 21 women, 28 men) via an online
survey platform. The adults saw all the vignettes and questions as the
children.

Unless otherwise noted, the studies' designs and primary analyses were
pre-registered (Studies 1, 3.A, and 4.A: <https://osf.io/pv9gj/>;
Studies 2, 3.B, and 4.B: <https://osf.io/sx37c/>).

**General analyses.** We conducted one-sided Bayesian binomial tests
with default priors, in the pre-registered direction of our hypotheses,
to determine whether participants chose to share the emotion over the
fact more often (or, for Study 4.B, less often) than expected by chance
in the forced-choice questions. We also conducted Bayesian probit
generalized linear mixed-effects models with default priors. For the
child samples we fit the pre-registered model, in which the condition
(happy/sad, and in relevant cases relationship type) was crossed with
the child's age; for the adult samples, who have no comparable age
variation, we fit a condition-only model. Participant ID was included as
a random effect. Where the condition × age interaction was not credible,
we refit the model without age and report the whole-group effect (full
developmental analyses in the Supplement). We assessed effects by
whether 0 fell within the 95% credible interval. We report additional
analyses --- including analyses of child gender (all null) and the
social-closeness (circles) measure --- in the Supplement.

**Transparency and openness.** We report how we determined our sample
sizes, all data exclusions, all manipulations, and all measures in the
studies, and we follow the Journal Article Reporting Standards [JARS;
@appelbaum2018jars]. Sample sizes were determined in advance: we
pre-registered a target of 56 children per wave, based on prior work
that detected comparable effects [@woo2024]; recruitment for the second
wave exceeded this target (see Supplement). Analyses were conducted in R
[@rcoreteam2025] using the brms package [@burkner2018brms]. All data,
analysis code, and study materials are available on the Open Science
Framework at \[OSF PROJECT URL\]; the pre-registrations are linked
above.

# Study 1: Inferences about social closeness based on information disclosure

In Study 1, children and adults were told about two different
protagonists at school. In one story, a protagonist learned an animal
fact and also felt sad at school. In the other story, a different
protagonist learned a different animal fact and felt happy at school
(the animal facts were borrowed from @shields2024). In each story, the
protagonist then encountered two classmates. The protagonists disclosed
their emotions but withheld the fact from one classmate and disclosed
the fact but withheld their emotions from the other classmate.

After the stories, as an attention check, children and adults were asked
to recall which character was told the fact and which character was told
the emotion. We then measured intuitions about social closeness across
two measures. Children and adults were asked which classmate they
thought was best friends with the protagonist. Finally, we had two
food-sharing items, one that required physical intimacy and one that did
not: children and adults were asked to guess who the protagonist would
want to share a single ice cream cone with, and who the protagonist
would share partitionable candy with. Based on prior work in which
children do not expect preferential sharing with family over friends,
but do expect physical intimacy with family compared to friends. We
reasoned that if children associate disclosing sad emotions with social
closeness, they should expect the protagonist to share the single ice
cream cone more often with the person they disclosed a sad emotion with,
which requires physical intimacy. On the other hand, if children think
that close social relationships are primarily about being more
prosocial, they should expect more sharing of the partitionable candy
with the person they inferred as closer. We also included a graded
measure of social closeness, in which children chose from a series of
circles that varied in how much they overlapped. Because its warm-up
trials involved only grossly different relationships (a parent and
child, friends, and strangers) rather than a finer, same-tier contrast
(such as a friend vs. a best friend), this measure may not have been
sensitive enough to detect the more subtle difference between the two
classmates; the full analyses and discussion are in the supplementary
materials. The design of Study 1, alongside the other three studies, is
summarized in @fig-overview.

## Results

**Better Friend.** When asked who was better friends with the
protagonist, children chose the person who learned about the
protagonist's emotion more often in the sad condition
(`r pct("study1","child","better_friend","sad")`,
`r bf("study1","child","better_friend","sad")`), but not in the happy
condition (`r pct("study1","child","better_friend","happy")`,
`r bf("study1","child","better_friend","happy")`). We tested whether
condition (happy/sad) affected children's answers using a Bayesian
probit mixed-effects model. The condition affected children's responses
(`r wg("study1_bf","b_emotionsad")`). Thus, children thought that
sharing a sad emotion was indicative of social closeness. By contrast,
adults chose the target of emotional disclosure across both the happy
and sad conditions (sad condition:
`r pct("study1","adult","better_friend","sad")`,
`r bf("study1","adult","better_friend","sad")`; happy condition
`r pct("study1","adult","better_friend","happy")`,
`r bf("study1","adult","better_friend","happy")`). Adults did not differ
across happy and sad conditions
(`r est("study1_bf_adult","b_emotionsad")`).

**Food sharing.** Next, we asked if children and adults used disclosing
emotions to predict food-sharing behavior. For the ice cream trial, in
the sad condition, children selected the character who was told the
emotion more often than the character who was told a fact
(`r pct("study1","child","ice_cream","sad")`,
`r bf("study1","child","ice_cream","sad")`). However, they chose each
character equally often in the happy condition
(`r pct("study1","child","ice_cream","happy")`,
`r bf("study1","child","ice_cream","happy")`). For the candy trials, we
did not find evidence that children chose either character more
frequently (sad condition: `r pct("study1","child","candy","sad")`,
`r bf("study1","child","candy","sad")`; happy condition:
`r pct("study1","child","candy","happy")`,
`r bf("study1","child","candy","happy")`). Consistent with their
inferences about who were 'best friends', adults selected the classmate
who was told the emotion in the sad condition
(`r pct("study1","adult","ice_cream","sad")`,
`r bf("study1","adult","ice_cream","sad")`) and in the happy condition
(`r pct("study1","adult","ice_cream","happy")`,
`r bf("study1","adult","ice_cream","happy")`) more often than the
classmate who was told the fact. Like children, adults were at chance in
their predictions about sharing candy (sad:
`r pct("study1","adult","candy","sad")`,
`r bf("study1","adult","candy","sad")`; happy:
`r pct("study1","adult","candy","happy")`,
`r bf("study1","adult","candy","happy")`).

We conducted Bayesian probit mixed-effects models to test an interaction
between item type and condition. For children, there was no main effect
of happy/sad conditions (`r wg("study1_food","b_emotionsad")`); nor of
food item (`r wg("study1_food","b_measureice_cream")`). There was also
no evidence of an interaction
(`r wg("study1_food","b_emotionsad:measureice_cream")`). For the adults,
there was no main effect of happy/sad conditions
(`r est("study1_food_adult","b_emotionsad")`); however, there was a main
effect of item (`r est("study1_food_adult","b_measureice_cream")`).
There was no evidence of an interaction
(`r est("study1_food_adult","b_emotionsad:measureice_cream")`).

In an exploratory analysis, because we were underpowered to find an
interaction, we fit a cell-means model treating each of the four
food-sharing conditions separately, with the happy/candy condition as
the reference. For children, the clearest departure from happy/candy was
the sad/ice cream condition
(`r est("study1_cellmeans_child","b_cond4sad_ice_cream")`), whereas
sad/candy (`r est("study1_cellmeans_child","b_cond4sad_candy")`) and
happy/ice cream
(`r est("study1_cellmeans_child","b_cond4happy_ice_cream")`) were closer
to the reference. For adults, both sad/ice cream
(`r est("study1_cellmeans_adult","b_cond4sad_ice_cream")`) and happy/ice
cream (`r est("study1_cellmeans_adult","b_cond4happy_ice_cream")`)
departed from happy/candy, but sad/candy did not
(`r est("study1_cellmeans_adult","b_cond4sad_candy")`). Refer to
@fig-study1 (panel A).

```{r}
#| label: fig-study1
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "Study 1. (A) Proportion of judgments choosing the classmate who was told the emotion (vs. the fact), by emotion condition and measure, for children and adults; dashed line = chance; * BF₁₀ ≥ 3, ** ≥ 10, *** ≥ 100. (B) Predicted probability of choosing the emotion for the better-friend judgment across age, by condition. (C) Predicted probability of choosing the emotion for food sharing across age, by condition and item. In (B) and (C), solid lines are model estimates for children and dashed lines are adult observed means."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "combined_study1.png"))
```

**Developmental change.** In the pre-registered probit model, the
emotion × age interaction was uncertain for the better-friend judgment
(`r est("study1_bf_child","b_emotionsad:age_c")`) and for food sharing
(`r est("study1_food_child","b_emotionsad:age_c")`); see @fig-study1
(panels B and C). Descriptively, for the single ice cream cone children
chose the classmate told the sad emotion at consistently high rates
across ages (about 70% from age 6 onward), whereas in the happy
condition this preference was near chance among the youngest children
and rose with age, approaching the sad pattern by age 9. This
corresponds to a three-way emotion × item × age interaction that was not
credible
(`r est("study1_food_child","b_emotionsad:measureice_cream:age_c")`), so
we interpret the difference cautiously.

## Discussion

In Study 1, we found evidence that children inferred closeness from
emotional disclosure. When the protagonist disclosed that they were sad
to one person and a piece of information to another, children said the
person who they disclosed the sad emotion to was the better friend. They
also connected emotional intimacy with physical intimacy, expecting this
person to share a single ice cream cone. By contrast, when the
protagonist disclosed a happy emotion, we did not find such effects.
This suggests that children view disclosing sadness as potentially more
vulnerable and indicative of social closeness. These findings provide
evidence that children are cognizant of the type of information
disclosed and can use that to infer social closeness. By contrast adults
did not distinguish between disclosing sad and happy emotions, always
choosing the classmate who was told the emotion over the fact as the
best friend. Thus, adults seem to have a more general intuition that
disclosing emotions is indicative of closeness, regardless of whether it
is sad or happy.

# Study 2: Using a close relationship to infer the type of information disclosed

In Study 1, we asked whether children and adults used the types of
information that protagonists disclose to infer social closeness. In
Study 2, we asked, given a close social relationship, do children and
adults predict that people would choose to disclose sad emotions? We
reasoned that if children and adults have an intuitive theory that
includes the connection between emotional disclosure and social
closeness, they should reason both ways.

In Study 2, children and adults learned about a protagonist who was
feeling sad or happy and encountered their friend and their best friend
separately. Participants were asked to choose if they thought the
protagonist would share the fact or their emotion with them. This
explores a type of close relationship that does not include family ties.

## Results

For children, we found moderate evidence that they expect the
protagonist to disclose a sad emotion to their best friend more than to
their friend (best friend: `r pct("study2","child","bestfriend","sad")`,
`r bf("study2","child","bestfriend","sad")`; friend:
`r pct("study2","child","friend","sad")`,
`r bf("study2","child","friend","sad")`). In the happy condition,
children were more likely than chance to expect the protagonist to
disclose the fact rather than a happy emotion to a friend
(`r pct("study2","child","friend","happy")`,
`r bf("study2","child","friend","happy")`), but were at chance for the
best friend (`r pct("study2","child","bestfriend","happy")`,
`r bf("study2","child","bestfriend","happy")`). We found a credible
effect of relationship (`r wg("study2","b_relationshipbestfriend")`):
children expected more emotional disclosure to a best friend than to a
friend. The effect of emotion condition trended in the expected
direction (`r wg("study2","b_emotionsad")`), but the interaction was not
credible (`r wg("study2","b_emotionsad:relationshipbestfriend")`).

For adults, we found strong evidence that they expect the protagonist to
disclose sad emotions over facts to a best friend
(`r pct("study2","adult","bestfriend","sad")`,
`r bf("study2","adult","bestfriend","sad")`). They did not have the same
expectation for a friend (`r pct("study2","adult","friend","sad")`,
`r bf("study2","adult","friend","sad")`). Conversely, adults seemed to
think the protagonist would disclose facts to their best friend
(`r pct("study2","adult","bestfriend","happy")`,
`r bf("study2","adult","bestfriend","happy")`) and friend
(`r pct("study2","adult","friend","happy")`,
`r bf("study2","adult","friend","happy")`) in the happy condition. The
model showed an effect of emotion condition
(`r est("study2_adult","b_emotionsad")`) but not relationship
(`r est("study2_adult","b_relationshipbestfriend")`). Refer to
@fig-study2 (panel A).

```{r}
#| label: fig-study2
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "Study 2. (A) Proportion of judgments that the protagonist would disclose the emotion (vs. the fact) to a friend vs. a best friend, by emotion condition, for children and adults. (B) Predicted probability of choosing the emotion across age, by condition and relationship; solid lines are model estimates for children, dashed lines are adult observed means, and points are individual children."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "combined_study2.png"))
```

**Developmental change.** The tendency to expect sad-emotion disclosure
to a best friend (vs. a friend) strengthened with age (three-way emotion
× relationship × age:
`r est("study2_child","b_emotionsad:relationshipbestfriend:age_c")`;
@fig-study2, panel B). By 9 years children strongly expected a sad
emotion to be shared with the best friend, whereas at 6 years they
showed little differentiation (see Supplement, Table S11).

## Discussion

In Study 2, children expected more emotional disclosure to a best friend
than to a friend. This aligns with literature suggesting that children
consider the relationship and use cues of social closeness when deciding
what information will be disclosed [@anagnostaki2013]. The effect of
emotion valence trended in the same direction as in Study 1---sad
emotions disclosed more than happy---but was not credible on its own,
though the per-age analysis showed the sad/best-friend preference
emerging with age. Adults showed the same best-friend preference
specifically for sad emotions, however unlike in Study 1 did
differentiate between the sad and happy condition. Thus, during the
period between 6 and 9 years children begin to differentiate a best
friend from a friend in their expectations about emotional disclosure,
looking more adult-like with age.

# Study 3: Using emotional disclosure to create and advance social closeness

## Study 3.A

In Studies 1 and 2, children and adults connected close social
relationships with emotional intimacy. It stood to reason, then, that
children would be able to use this information when thinking about how
another person might try to create a social relationship. This would
speak to an intuitive theory of relationships that grants children and
adults the opportunity to intervene in the world by using a causal model
of how relationships and emotional intimacy coincide. In Study 3, we
investigate this possibility.

### Methods

In Study 3.A, children and adults learned about a protagonist who wanted
to become close friends with a character they met for the first time. We
then asked the participants to choose whether the protagonist should
disclose the fact or their emotional state to achieve this goal.

### Results

We found that the children were equally likely to say that the
protagonist should disclose a fact compared to an emotion across both
the sad condition (`r pct("study3A","child","create","sad")` chose
emotion, `r bf("study3A","child","create","sad")`) and the happy
condition (`r pct("study3A","child","create","happy")` chose emotion,
`r bf("study3A","child","create","happy")`). Adults answered similarly
across conditions (sad: `r pct("study3A","adult","create","sad")`,
`r bf("study3A","adult","create","sad")`; happy:
`r pct("study3A","adult","create","happy")`,
`r bf("study3A","adult","create","happy")`). We also did not find
evidence of effects of condition in our model for the children
(`r wg("study3A","b_emotionsad")`) or for the adults
(`r est("study3A_adult","b_emotionsad")`).

**Developmental change.** The emotion × age interaction was not credible
(`r est("study3A_child","b_emotionsad:age_c")`; @fig-study3-age, panel
A).

## Study 3.B

In Study 3.B, children and adults learned that the protagonist has a
friend with whom they want to become best friends. In order to achieve
this, we asked the participants if the protagonist would tell about the
fact or their emotion.

### Results

We pre-registered the hypothesis that people would disclose sad emotions
in order to deepen an existing friendship, so the one-sided tests asked
whether the emotion was chosen more than chance. Children did not show
this pattern: in the sad condition they were no more likely than chance
to choose the emotion over the fact
(`r pct("study3B","child","deepen","sad")`,
`r bf("study3B","child","deepen","sad")`), nor in the happy condition
(`r pct("study3B","child","deepen","happy")`,
`r bf("study3B","child","deepen","happy")`); the model showed no effect
of emotion condition (`r wg("study3B","b_emotionsad")`). For adults, the
evidence ran clearly against the hypothesis: they did not expect the
protagonist to disclose sad emotions to become closer to a friend
(`r pct("study3B","adult","deepen","sad")`,
`r bf("study3B","adult","deepen","sad")`), nor happy emotions
(`r pct("study3B","adult","deepen","happy")`,
`r bf("study3B","adult","deepen","happy")`); the condition effect in the
model was `r est("study3B_adult","b_emotionsad")`.

**Developmental change.** The emotion × age interaction was not credible
(`r est("study3B_child","b_emotionsad:age_c")`; @fig-study3-age, panel
B).

```{r}
#| label: fig-study3-age
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "Study 3 developmental change. Predicted probability of choosing the emotion (vs. the fact) across age, by condition, for (A) Study 3.A (creating a new relationship) and (B) Study 3.B (deepening an existing friendship). Solid lines are model estimates for children; dashed lines are adult observed means."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "combined_study3_age.png"))
```

## Discussion

Study 3.A aimed to investigate whether children and adults thought that
cultivating emotional intimacy was one way to create a relationship. We
found that neither children nor adults had this intuition. This suggests
that, while children and adults think revealing sad emotions occurs in
established close relationships, it may not be a tool to create
relationships.

Similarly, in Study 3.B, children and adults did not have strong
intuitions about disclosing sad or happy emotions for a previously
established friendship to become closer. These results were a little
surprising because we thought that the extra information about the
relationship would lead the participants to infer that disclosing
sensitive emotions and displaying vulnerability may be a way to deepen a
relationship. In Study 4 we investigate how children reason about
emotional disclosure in an intimate, but asymmetrical relationships, a
parent child relationship [@thomas2025caregiving]

# Study 4: Emotional disclosure in asymmetrical relationships

One reason people may withhold sad emotions in some relationships is
that disclosing sadness implies the listener should help or comfort the
discloser. This implication is not only about closeness but about the
structure of a relationship---it matters who is able or expected to
provide care. The mother--child relationship offers a useful test of
this idea, because it is unambiguously close yet asymmetric in
caregiving: the mother is ordinarily the one who comforts a child, not
the reverse. In Study 4 we examined emotional disclosure in this
asymmetric relationship from both directions. In Study 4.A, we asked
whether children expect a child to disclose sad emotions to their own
mother, a close relationship, but not to a friend's mother, a more
distant relationship. This comparison tests whether children expect
emotional disclosure within close relationships, but it leaves open
whether they treat emotional disclosure as a symmetric feature of
closeness, something both partners do or as something that can also
align with the caregiving structure of a relationship. In Study 4.B we
therefore tested the reverse direction: whether children expect a mother
to disclose her own sadness to her child. If children expect emotional
disclosure simply because a relationship is close, they should expect a
mother to disclose her sadness to her child, just as they expect a child
to disclose to a mother. If instead children intuit that a mother
disclosing sadness would imply that her young child ought to comfort
her, inverting their roles, they should be less inclined to expect that
a mother will share her sad emotion, even though the relationship is
close.

## Study 4.A

### Methods

As in Study 2, participants were told that a protagonist's mother and a
friend's mother asked how the protagonist's day was at school. We then
asked participants to predict whether the protagonist would disclose the
fact or their emotional state across the happy and sad conditions.

### Results

In the sad condition, children predicted the protagonist would disclose
the emotion more often than the fact with their mother
(`r pct("study4A","child","mom","sad")`,
`r bf("study4A","child","mom","sad")`). Children did not predict that
the protagonist would disclose the sad emotion more often with the
friend's mother (`r pct("study4A","child","friendmom","sad")` predicted
the emotion, `r bf("study4A","child","friendmom","sad")`). In the happy
condition, children were at chance in both relationship conditions
(mother: `r pct("study4A","child","mom","happy")`,
`r bf("study4A","child","mom","happy")`; friend's mother:
`r pct("study4A","child","friendmom","happy")`,
`r bf("study4A","child","friendmom","happy")`). Adults predicted the
protagonist to disclose sad emotion with their mother more than the fact
(`r pct("study4A","adult","mom","sad")`,
`r bf("study4A","adult","mom","sad")`), but happy feelings were closer
to chance (`r pct("study4A","adult","mom","happy")`,
`r bf("study4A","adult","mom","happy")`). They did not expect the
protagonist to disclose emotions with the friend's mom (sad:
`r pct("study4A","adult","friendmom","sad")`,
`r bf("study4A","adult","friendmom","sad")`; happy:
`r pct("study4A","adult","friendmom","happy")`,
`r bf("study4A","adult","friendmom","happy")`).

We also conducted a Bayesian probit mixed-effects model. For children,
the happy/sad condition interacted with relationship
(`r wg("study4A","b_emotionsad:relationshipmom")`). In agreement with
the results of Study 1, it seems that children expect that people
disclose sad emotions more often in close relationships than in other
positive relationships. They don't make the same inferences when the
emotion is happy. For the adults, relationship affected responses
(`r est("study4A_adult","b_relationshipmom")`), while emotional
condition did not (`r est("study4A_adult","b_emotionsad")`), with mixed
evidence for an interaction
(`r est("study4A_adult","b_emotionsad:relationshipmom")`). Refer to
@fig-study4a (panel A).

```{r}
#| label: fig-study4a
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "Study 4.A. (A) Proportion of judgments that the protagonist would disclose the emotion (vs. the fact) to their mother vs. a friend's mother, by emotion condition, for children and adults. (B) Predicted probability of choosing the emotion across age, by condition and relationship; solid lines are model estimates for children and dashed lines are adult observed means."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "combined_study4A.png"))
```

**Developmental change.** We did not find evidence for age effects: the
three-way emotion × relationship × age interaction was not credible
(`r est("study4A_child","b_emotionsad:relationshipmom:age_c")`;
@fig-study4a, panel B).

## Study 4.B

Study 4.B reversed the direction of disclosure used in Study 4.A.
Participants were told that the protagonist's mother---and, separately,
a friend's mother---had learned a fact at work and was feeling happy or
sad, and that the protagonist asked how her day was. We then asked
participants to predict whether the mother would disclose the fact or
her emotional state.

### Results

Because we pre-registered that the mother would not share sad emotions
with her child, the one-sided binomial tests in this study asked whether
the fact was chosen more often than chance (i.e., evidence against
emotional disclosure). For children, we found that they generally did
not expect the protagonist's mother
(`r pct("study4B","child","mom","sad")`,
`r bf("study4B","child","mom","sad")`) or the friend's mother
(`r pct("study4B","child","friendmom","sad")`,
`r bf("study4B","child","friendmom","sad")`) to disclose sad emotions to
the protagonist. Children also did not expect the protagonist's mother
(`r pct("study4B","child","mom","happy")`,
`r bf("study4B","child","mom","happy")`) or friend's mother
(`r pct("study4B","child","friendmom","happy")`,
`r bf("study4B","child","friendmom","happy")`) to disclose happy
emotions. After running the model, we found no effect of emotion
condition (`r wg("study4B","b_emotionsad")`) or relationship
(`r wg("study4B","b_relationshipmom")`), with inconclusive evidence for
the interaction (`r wg("study4B","b_emotionsad:relationshipmom")`).

Adults had similar intuitions; there was inconclusive evidence that they
expected the protagonist's mother to disclose sad emotions over facts
(`r pct("study4B","adult","mom","sad")`,
`r bf("study4B","adult","mom","sad")`). However, they expected the
friend's mother to share facts over sad emotions
(`r pct("study4B","adult","friendmom","sad")`,
`r bf("study4B","adult","friendmom","sad")`). Similarly, there was
inconclusive evidence whether adults expected the mother to disclose
happy emotions (`r pct("study4B","adult","mom","happy")`,
`r bf("study4B","adult","mom","happy")`), and they did not expect the
friend's mother to disclose happy emotions
(`r pct("study4B","adult","friendmom","happy")`,
`r bf("study4B","adult","friendmom","happy")`). We found no effects of
condition (`r est("study4B_adult","b_emotionsad")`) or relationship
(`r est("study4B_adult","b_relationshipmom")`). Refer to @fig-study4b
(panel A).

```{r}
#| label: fig-study4b
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "Study 4.B. (A) Proportion of judgments that the mother / friend's mother would disclose the emotion (vs. the fact) to the protagonist, by emotion condition, for children and adults. (B) Predicted probability of choosing the emotion across age, by condition and relationship; solid lines are model estimates for children and dashed lines are adult observed means."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "combined_study4B.png"))
```

**Developmental change.** Older children were less likely to expect a
parent to disclose sad emotions (emotion × age:
`r est("study4B_child","b_emotionsad:age_c")`; @fig-study4b, panel B).

## Discussion

In Study 4.A, we found that children expected the protagonist to tell
their mother that they felt sad at school over telling her about a fact.
This was not true for a friend's mother. When the protagonist had been
feeling happy, children did not seem to have clear expectations about
what should be disclosed with whom. This supports the hypothesis that
children recognize mother--child as a close relationship and expect
emotional intimacy in this relationship. The adults had a similar
pattern, but like Study 1 did not as strongly distinguish between happy
and sad emotions. Like children, the adults also expected the
protagonist to disclose sad emotions to their mother, but were trending
toward saying that the child would also share happy emotions. In
contrast to children, they strongly expected that children would be more
likely to share facts than emotions, regardless of the emotion, with the
friend's mother (

However, in Study 4.B, children and adults did not expect that the
protagonist's mother or a friend's mother would share emotions with the
protagonist. In contrast to the results of Study 4.A, where participants
expected the protagonist to disclose sad emotions to their mother, both
children and adults seem to have intuitions that emotional disclosure
does not go both ways in asymmetrical relationships, as they do not hold
similar expectations when the roles are reversed.

# General Discussion

In four studies, we examined 6- to 9-year-old U.S. American children's
and U.S. American adults' expectations about the connection between
disclosing information and close relationships. In Study 1, the
participants learned about a protagonist who disclosed a fact to one
character and failed to disclose how they had been feeling at school.
That same character disclosed an emotion they had been feeling to a
different character but omitted the fact. We varied whether the emotion
was happy or sad and found that children inferred that the character who
was told about the sad emotion, as opposed to the fact, was better
friends with the protagonist. This was not true when the character could
have disclosed a happy emotion as opposed to a fact. Children and adults
also connected emotional disclosure with physical intimacy: they
expected that the protagonist would share a single ice cream cone, but
not partitionable candy, with the character who had been told about the
sad emotion. Thus, children seemed to use emotional intimacy as a cue of
social closeness. By contrast, adults inferred that the characters who
were told either emotion were better friends with the protagonist and
were more likely to share physical intimacy with the protagonist.

In Study 2, we asked if children and adults used relationships to make
predictions about emotional disclosure. Children expected the
protagonist to share emotions more often with best friends and to share
more facts with friends, and this was especially pronounced in the sad
condition. We also found a three-way interaction with age, suggesting
that as children got older they became more likely to predict sharing a
sad emotion with best friends over friends. Despite the results of Study
1, where adults did not distinguish between sad and happy emotions, in
Study 2 adults were more likely to predict disclosing a sad emotion to a
best friend than a fact, but did not make the same predictions in the
happy condition.

In Study 3, we asked whether children and adults would use this
knowledge to make predictions about how someone would act if they wanted
to create or deepen a relationship. Here, contrary to our hypotheses,
neither children nor adults made systematic predictions; if anything,
they predicted that the protagonist would share facts as opposed to
emotions.

In Study 4, we asked how children think about emotional disclosure in
asymmetric relationships. We compared participants' intuitions about
sharing between the child protagonist and their mother, and the
protagonist and their friend's mother. Children guessed that the
protagonist would disclose sadness with their mother, but not happiness,
and did not expect the protagonist to disclose emotions with their
friend's mother. Like Study 2, and again in contrast to Study 1, adults
distinguished between happy and sad emotions, expecting the protagonist
to disclose sad emotions with their mother more than happy emotions.
Like children, they did not expect the protagonist to share either
emotion with a friend's mom. Next we investigated whether children and
adults recognized that the asymmetric nature of these relationships
would lead to different patterns of disclosure, asking whether the
protagonist's mother and friend's mother would disclose emotions to the
protagonist. Neither children nor adults expected them to. Together,
this suggests that both adults' and children's intuitions about
emotional disclosure are driven not only by closeness, but also by
asymmetry in close social relationships.

These studies provide insights into children's intuitive theories of
social relationships and how disclosure may play into these theories.
Importantly, across these studies, children compared different ways a
person can talk about their day (either disclosing information they
learned or disclosing an emotional experience they had). Interestingly,
disclosing a fact could be framed as more prosocial, as it could benefit
the recipient, while disclosing an emotion would not benefit the
receiver. Yet children did not see disclosing a fact as oppposed to an
emotion as indicative of close social relationships.

These findings add to the growing literature that children distinguish
between intimate, close or thick relationships from affiliative or thin
relationships within the larger category of prosocial relationships.
While the children in these studies had years of experience with
different kinds of relationships, it is still striking that relatively
young children recognize emotional intimacy. It seems unlikely that they
have been explicitly taught that people only disclose sad or negative
emotions with close others, nor that you can tell who is close based on
what they disclose. Thus, it is an interesting question how children
come to have this knowledge. Children may have learned it through more
subtle cues (for example, social awkwardness can arise when someone
'overshares') or they might have learned this association from
situations where emotionally disclosing to close others meant that they
provided empathy in a way that others cannot (refer to
@crockett2025empathy). Children may intuit a process whereby they
understand that disclosing negative emotions can make the discloser seem
less desirable as a social partner or imply that the target of
disclosure should help.

This may be why neither adults nor children expected people to disclose
negative emotions to create a new relationship nor to deepen an existing
one. This pattern---that adults and children perceived emotional
disclosure as a marker of existing closeness rather than a device for
creating it---resonates with classical sociological accounts of how
intimacy works. @simmel1906sociology held that self-disclosure is one
way that close relationships develop but argued that intimacy
simultaneously depends on reserve: too much disclosure can threaten
social bonds. On this view, disclosing sad emotions to create a
relationship would be premature, consistent with our finding that
neither children nor adults treated disclosing sadness as a way to
create a new friendship or to deepen one. Rather, the relationship may
already need to feel close for people to intuit that one should disclose
sad emotions. This past theory also agree with our results of Studies 2
and 4, where emotional disclosure was expected within an already-close
relationship (a mother / best friend) but not toward a more distant one
(a friend's mother / friend). However, children did not strongly expect
mothers to disclose sad emotions to their children. This agrees with
past work theorizing that part of the risk of disclosing sad emotions is
the implication that someone should help, which may be inappropriate in
less close relations, or in caregiving relationships when one individual
has the role of caregiver.

One caveat to the current studies is that this intuition may be
relatively later developing compared to other intuitions about social
closeness, such as responses to apparent distress and physical intimacy
that involves close physical contact [@thomasSaliva2022]. In some of our
studies we found age effects and in others we did not. Our age effects,
and overall findings agree with previous work in this domain, in which
children inferred social closeness after learning that a character
correctly predicted how their social partner would feel and expected
close others to better know the preferences of their social partners
[@woo2024]. The current studies suggest that at least one explanation
for these previous findings is the intuition that people are more likely
to share mental states, especially negative emotional states, with close
social partners, and may even hide those mental states from less close
social partners. However, children in those previous studies did not
seem to systematically differentiate between sad and happy emotions.
Thus, the current results are likely one piece of a larger puzzle about
people's intuitions about knowledge of others and social closeness.

It is also unclear why adults did not consistently differentiate between
happy and sad conditions, while children did. First, it should be noted
that we did not directly pit sad emotions against happy emotions. It is
possible that while adults see all emotional disclosure as part of close
relationships, they may expect that a person who was told about sadness,
compared to a person told about happiness, would be the closer friend.
For this reason, it is possible that both children and adults
distinguish between disclosing sadness and happiness but that our
studies were not designed well to find this contrast. Perhaps adults
intuited that sharing a fun fact with anyone, but people who are not
close may care less about one's inner life, where children may be less
conscious of such differences.

Future research could further investigate why adults and children
differed in this regard and why children differentiate between happy and
sad emotions. For example, do children intuit that a negative emotion
could reflect badly on the discloser? Or could it be because disclosing
a negative emotion can be unpleasant for the listener or imply an
obligation to help? Perhaps, while it is sometimes unpleasant or
draining to learn about other people's emotional states, in many close
social relationships we either don't mind because we value the other
person's emotional well-being, and/or because we simply want to know
what is going on with our close social partners.

One major limitation of these findings is the relatively homogeneous
pool of participants in a particular cultural context. Future studies
could investigate whether these expectations vary on demographics and
culture. Cross-cultural work suggests that how readily people talk about
others' mental states varies. For example, among the Ku Waru of highland
Papua New Guinea parents and children spontaneously refer to specific
beliefs and desires far less frequently than comparable North American
parent--child pairs, this may lead children to learn that disclosing
emotional states is not a reliable cue of social closeness, or may
exacerbate it if it is done even more infrequently [@rumsey2013opacity].
In some cultural contexts gender may play a role in whether and which
emotions are discussed and with whom. It could also be fruitful to
examine how children determine when a relationship is close enough to
involve emotional intimacy. For example, we pitted disclosing emotions
against facts, but what would happen if we investigated different
person-specific mental states such as preferences? What other types of
information disclosure might lead to inferences about social closeness?
While many questions remain, the current series of studies is one step
closer to understanding how children connect relationships to patterns
of information disclosure.

## Constraints on Generality

We expect these results to generalize to U.S. American children between
the ages of 6 and 9 years and to U.S. American adults, the populations
from which our samples were drawn. Participants were recruited online
through a laboratory database, social media, Children Helping Science,
and Prolific; the child samples were predominantly White and drawn from
relatively highly educated families, and the adult samples were young
(mean ages of roughly 23 to 27 years). We have no strong reason to
believe that the basic association between emotional disclosure and
social closeness depends on these particular characteristics. However,
the more specific patterns we observed---especially the reliance on sad
emotions as the diagnostic cue, and the asymmetry we found in caregiving
relationships---may depend on culturally specific norms about how, and
how much, people discuss their inner states [@rumsey2013opacity]. We
therefore do not assume that these findings extend to children or adults
in cultural contexts with different conventions surrounding emotional
expression, to children younger than those we tested, or to relationship
types beyond those we examined (best friends, friends, and mother--child
dyads). We see no reason to expect that the results depend on the
specific experimenters, stimuli, or online testing platforms used,
though this remains to be established.

# Author Note

\[Author names and affiliations removed for anonymous review.\]

This research was supported by a research fellowship \[funder details
removed for anonymous review\]. The authors declare no conflicts of
interest. The studies' designs and primary analyses were pre-registered
(Studies 1, 3.A, and 4.A: \[pre-registration link removed for anonymous
review\]; Studies 2, 3.B, and 4.B: \[pre-registration link removed for
anonymous review\]), and all data, analysis code, and study materials
are available at \[anonymized repository link\].

\[Acknowledgments removed for anonymous review.\]

\[Correspondence information removed for anonymous review.\]

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
